weather: Today in Edmonds: Morning low clouds breaking to partly cloudy skies, high 57°F. Light SSE winds 6-9 mph. 20% chance of sprinkles midday. Tonight increasing clouds, low 47°F. Pleasant spring day—jacket weather for Piper's stroller walk, no umbrella needed.

local_news.findings: The City of Edmonds will begin construction on its 2025 Combined Cured-in-Place Pipe (CIPP) Rehabilitation Project on May 4, 2026. This major infrastructure project will repair aging sewer and storm pipes at 27 locations throughout the city, addressing failing infrastructure identified in a 2013 review. The project represents a significant investment in municipal utilities and will impact local traffic patterns during construction.

The City of Edmonds hosted an open house on Thursday, April 24, 2026 to share the 60% design plan for Stage 3 of the Highway 99 Revitalization Project. This represents continued progress on the multi-phase revitalization effort for the Highway 99 corridor, with public input being solicited on the current design phase. The project aims to improve infrastructure and aesthetics along this major arterial route through Edmonds.

Recent police activity in Edmonds includes a theft arrest of an adult female at the 21900 block of Highway 99, who was booked into Lynnwood Jail. Additionally, an adult male was arrested on an outstanding warrant. These incidents represent the typical public safety activity in the area, with Highway 99 continuing to be a location where law enforcement maintains regular presence.

The South County Fire Board of Commissioners is actively working on redistricting plans to incorporate Edmonds' 42,000+ residents into the Regional Fire Authority. Two competing proposals are under consideration: Plan 1 would keep most of Edmonds in a single district, while Plan 2 would split the city between districts. The board is seeking public input before making a final decision, with implications for how Edmonds residents will be represented on the fire commission.

global_news.findings: Israel's complete blockade of Gaza is being challenged at the International Court of Justice, with the UN accusing Israel of using aid as a 'weapon of war' and violating international law. Since March 2, Israel has cut off all supplies to Gaza's 2.3 million residents, leading to severe food shortages and starvation conditions. The World Court hearings represent a major legal escalation in the Israel-Palestine conflict.

Germany announced a dramatic shift in military policy, pledging to become 'the backbone of conventional defence in Europe' with a 500 billion euro ($569 billion) defense spending surge. President Frank-Walter Steinmeier declared that 'a badly armed Germany is a greater threat to Europe than a strongly armed Germany,' marking a historic reversal of post-WWII military restraint as Europe faces uncertainty over US defense commitments.

King Charles III arrived in the US for a high-profile state visit overshadowed by the Iran war and security concerns following the White House Correspondents' Dinner shooting. The visit comes amid strained UK-US relations and ongoing geopolitical tensions across the Middle East.

Hezbollah and Israel exchanged threats and military strikes across Lebanon's border, escalating tensions in the region. The cross-border hostilities represent a dangerous flashpoint as the broader Middle East conflict continues to intensify.

The UK government announced new measures to exclude foreign sex offenders from receiving refugee protections, part of Prime Minister Keir Starmer's broader immigration crackdown. The policy change reflects growing political pressure over asylum and border security issues.

Gunmen kidnapped 23 children from a Nigerian orphanage in the latest mass abduction targeting vulnerable populations. The incident highlights the ongoing security crisis in Nigeria, where kidnapping has become a lucrative criminal enterprise.

intellectual_journals.findings: Maria Popova's essay 'Why You' explores the philosophical question of selfhood as a narrative construct built from cosmic chance. She argues that identity is 'a selective retelling of the myriad chance events between the birth of the universe and this moment,' emphasizing how our existence is shaped by arbitrary variables like 'atoms bonding one way and not another.' The piece draws on Iris Murdoch's observation that 'the self... is a place of illusion' and Blaise Pascal's existential terror at the randomness of existence, ultimately suggesting that love becomes the counterforce to cosmic help [TRUNCATED]

In 'Movements Need the Critical Thinking That AI Destroys,' Jacobin examines how AI chatbots create a form of 'decaffeinated subjectivity' by outsourcing human judgment to algorithms. The piece cites an MIT study showing 'significantly reduced brain activity among regular users of chatbots' and warns this represents a new kind of existential risk—not AI rebellion, but the gradual erosion of independent thought necessary for democratic participation. The essay connects this to Kant's 1784 essay 'What Is Enlightenment?' about freeing oneself from 'self-incurred immaturity,' arguing that AI syste [TRUNCATED]

Ian Penman's LRB essay 'I'm just a sound: Back to the Beach Boys' presents Brian Wilson's music as achieving a unique synthesis of pop hooks and sacred feeling. Penman describes how songs like 'God Only Knows' and 'Caroline, No' create the sensation of 'standing alone in a cathedral, bathed in sound,' arguing Wilson crafted 'secular hymns baited with pop hooks; heavy themes made exquisitely light.' The piece traces this to rock's origins in the tension between 'Saturday night and Sunday morning, church pew and dance floor,' suggesting Wilson's genius lay in making pop music that was 'uplifting [TRUNCATED]

Flora Champy's Aeon essay 'The role of literature as the key to personal freedom' examines Marcel Proust's defense of reading against John Ruskin's moralizing approach. The piece explores Proust's argument that books provide 'valuable insights into our lives' not through moral instruction but by preserving 'indelible impressions of our surroundings' and making 'time tangible: something to be grasped without being abolished.' Champy contrasts Ruskin's view that reading should train 'disinterestedness' against Victorian utilitarianism with Proust's more intimate understanding of literature as cr [TRUNCATED]

ProPublica's investigation 'Mass Arrests by Trump's ICE, CBP Repeatedly Fail Under Scrutiny' reveals how federal immigration enforcement has targeted protesters and bystanders through dubious conspiracy charges. The investigation identified over 300 arrests where cases 'fell apart under scrutiny' as officer statements were 'repeatedly debunked by video footage.' The piece documents how arrests like Alejandro Orellana's—charged with conspiracy under statutes typically used for drug traffickers—collapsed when searches found no incriminating evidence. Experts warn these arrests create a 'chilling [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: The AI research landscape in 2026 is being revolutionized by sophisticated multi-agent systems that can autonomously conduct end-to-end scientific research. Google's PaperOrchestra framework represents a breakthrough in automated research paper writing, using five specialized agents to transform raw experimental data into submission-ready LaTeX manuscripts with literature reviews and verified citations. The Auton Agentic AI Framework provides a principled architecture for autonomous agent systems, addressing the architectural mismatch between stochastic LLM outputs and deterministic backend requirements through a strict separation between cognitive blueprints and runtime engines. Meanwhile, the AI Scientist pipeline has achieved full end-to-end automation of the scientific process, from hypothesis generation through experimentation to paper writing. In astrophysics, the Cmbagent system demonstrated that semi-autonomous agent workflows can compete with expert solutions, achieving first- [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Congress is accelerating UAP disclosure with unprecedented specificity. Rep. Anna Paulina Luna's Task Force demanded 46 classified videos from the Pentagon by April 14, including footage of UAP formations over Iran, instant acceleration in Syrian airspace, and spherical objects entering water near US submarines. The White House has approved Rep. Eric Burlison's request to visit secret facilities where whistleblowers allege non-human craft are stored at contractors including Northrop Grumman and Lockheed Martin. A classified March 25 briefing in a SCIF has become central to the mystery surrounding missing aerospace scientists, including retired Air Force General Neil McCasland, who vanished after Trump's February disclosure order. The 2026 NDAA now mandates Pentagon briefings on UAP intercepts and requires AARO to account for all UAP classification guides.

newyorker.title: Ellen Burstyn’s Inner Library
newyorker.dek: Kris Kristofferson told her he was a poet when they co-starred in “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore.” Her new book tells the story of her life in poetry, Michael Schulman writes.
newyorker.text:
She wore green slacks, a green turtleneck, and a green cardigan (“I do like green”), with chunky metallic necklaces. In her writing room, lined with shelves of Mary Oliver and Rumi—her Oscar, for “Alice Doesn’t Live Here Anymore,” was across the hall—she showed off the desk where she wrote her new book, “Poetry Says It Better,” in which she revisits her life story through verses she has loved. “I like what metaphors set off in your mind,” she said. “I like what poetry plays with.”
She settled into a wicker chair in the living room, beneath a wooden mermaid that she got in Mexico. Her cavapoo, Kerri, sat regally on a divan. The first poem that spoke to Burstyn, she recalled, was William Ernest Henley’s “Invictus,” which she read in high school, in Detroit. “ ‘I am the master of my fate, I am the captain of my soul’—that awakened an understanding in me that life was not always going to be the way it was.” At school, she was head cheerleader, but her home life was arduous. “My mother married a man who hated her two children,” she said. “I was being punished a lot physically.” She wrote her own poem and gave it to a boy in her class. “He said, ‘Just because your name is Edna’—which was my original name—‘doesn’t mean you have to copy her style.’ I said, ‘Who?’ ” That’s how she discovered Edna St. Vincent Millay. “It was like she was my voice,” she said, and rattled off Millay’s sonnet “Love Is Not All.”
At nineteen, Burstyn married her first husband, a poet and car salesman (“In Detroit, everybody’s involved with cars”), who introduced her to Baudelaire. “When I read Baudelaire now, I go, Boy, he’s really dark!” she said. By twenty-four, she was modelling in New York and decided to become an actress. “I went around saying, ‘I’m going to do a Broadway play this fall. Do you know how I can get an audition?’ ” It worked: she made her Broadway début in 1957, in the comedy “Fair Game.” Her determination put her in mind of the poem “For Longing,” by John O’Donohue: “May you have the courage to listen to the voice of desire.”
Some years later, in Hollywood, she was acting in the Debbie Reynolds flick “Goodbye Charlie.” “I said, ‘This is it. This is the big time. Next I’ll be playing Debbie Reynolds’s part!’ And this voice in my head said, I don’t want it,” she recalled. Through Shelley Winters, she met the acting teacher Lee Strasberg and studied with him. “I did a scene for him, and he said, ‘I don’t know, dear. You either laugh or you cry. If you would laugh and cry at the same time, then we’d see something.’ ” She found herself doing just that in her breakout film, “The Last Picture Show.” This reminded her of what the poet Wisława Szymborska wrote about the soul: “Joy and sorrow / aren’t two different feelings for it.”
Burstyn starred in “Alice” in 1974, alongside Kris Kristofferson, who insisted that he couldn’t act or sing. “What is it you do?” she remembered asking him. “I am a poet,” he answered. In her book, she included a sampling of his lyrics. (“He’s a walkin’ contradiction, partly truth and partly fiction.”) Did any poems remind her of starring in “The Exorcist”? Not quite, she said, but the film does open with the Muslim call to prayer. In the seventies, inspired by an etching of a chanting muezzin in her copy of “The Rubáiyát of Omar Khayyám,” she began travelling the Middle East. In Iran, she visited the Tomb of Hafez. “People come reading Hafez’s poetry,” she said, recalling a path lined with roses “about at nose height, so as you’re walking you’re sniffing a different rose each step.”
In 1995, she was in “How to Make an American Quilt”; her co-star Maya Angelou would recite poems for her. “The makeup department threw up their hands, because I had to be completely re-made up after lunch every day from crying in Maya’s trailer,” Burstyn recalled. “So then I snuck in and brought Q-tips.” She leaned back in her chair and mimed holding the cotton tips to her eyes. “I would lay down in her trailer with Q-tips and go, ‘O.K., hi [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.title: Movements Need the Critical Thinking That AI Destroys
enriched_articles.text:
# Movements Need the Critical Thinking That AI Destroys

Published: 2026-04-26T15:17:26+00:00

## Summary

Jacobin argues that the outsourcing of judgment to AI systems could lead to the gradual erosion of our capacity for independent thought. This is not the case of an apocalypse as AI becomes so powerful that it turns against human beings, but in relation to the question of what it means to be human. A recent MIT study found significantly reduced brain activity among regular users of chatbots, which supports this concern. The author suggests that chatbots represent a technologized form of subjectivity, a form of "decaffeinated" subjectivity. He argues that while these tools can provide significant efficiency, they could potentially threaten people’s participation in democratic processes and emancipatory politics.

## Story

Movements Need the Critical Thinking That AI Destroys Our special spring issue is out now. Get a discounted subscription to our print magazine today. Your support keeps us publishing! # Israel’s Young Settler Vanguard # The Making of the Teenager # Zohran Needs to Create Popular Assemblies Gabriel Hetland Bhaskar Sunkara # A Half-Century of Harry Braverman’s L [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.title: The role of literature as the key to personal freedom
enriched_articles.text:
# Does reading do us any good?
## Stripped of easy moralising, literature makes us relish the search for truth in an age when many believe truth to be dead

by Flora Champy + BIO

Does reading turn us into better people? Does it make us more sensitive and empathetic? Does it improve our judgment? And if it is not edifying, then what good does it do? About 120 years ago, the indecisive, dilettante offspring of a wealthy physician found these questions so important that he took up his pen to argue that, no, books are never the instruments of moral betterment. He based his argument on his own memories. Though he had always been an avid reader, books, he claimed, never gave him any sort of useful, respectable instruction. That does not mean they were meaningless – far from it; they shaped his apprehension of life by preserving indelible impressions of his surroundings. Relatives now long gone, places he hadn’t seen in years – these were nevertheless still present in his mind through memories of his readings. Books helped keep past sensations alive. Literature made time tangible: something to be grasped without being abolished.

Marcel Proust by Otto Wegener, c1895. Courtesy Wikimedia

 [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.title: Hezbollah and Israel swap threats and strikes across Lebanon's border
enriched_articles.text:
# Hezbollah and Israel swap threats and strikes across Lebanon’s border
*Iran-linked group reiterates defiance; Israeli defence minister threatens to ‘burn all of Lebanon’.*

Israel has threatened to “burn” all of Lebanon after Hezbollah reiterated its defiance and rejection of peace talks as war across the border grinds on.

The Lebanon-based armed group on Monday condemned Beirut’s ongoing discussions with Israel and again refused to end its campaign of strikes across the border in the face of Israel’s persisting attacks on the south of the country. Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz retorted that defiance would bring catastrophic consequences.

Hezbollah leader Naim Qassem asserted in a statement that while “sacrifices are great”, the Lebanese people have two options: “Liberation and pride or occupation and humiliation.”

The Iran-linked armed group has criticised the Lebanon government for entering talks with Israel to seek an end to the latest series of hostilities, which was sparked by the United States-Israeli war on Tehran.

“We are continuing our defensive resistance for Lebanon and its people,” Qassem said. “We will not return to the pre-March [status quo]; we will resp [TRUNCATED]
